## Setup


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
pacman::p_load(tidyverse, 
               here, 
               countdown, 
               tidytuesdayR, 
               lubridate, 
               janitor, 
               showtext, 
               scico, 
               DT, 
               readr,

               sf,
               ggmap,

               geofacet, 
               geosphere,
               glue,
               splitstackshape,
               countrycode,
               rnaturalearth,

               ggrepel
               )

# theme
ggplot2::theme_set(ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 14))
options(width = 65)

# figure defaults
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  fig.width  = 9,
  fig.asp    = 0.618,
  fig.retina = 3,
  fig.align  = "center",
  dpi        = 300
)
```
:::


# Question 1 - Global Language Patterns

What geographic patterns exist for endangered languages around the world?

## Part 1 - Endangered vs. Isolate Languages

What relationship exists between a language's isolate status and its endangered status?

##### What is a language isolate?

Per [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Language_isolate), a **language isolate**, sometimes called an **isolated language**, is a language that has no demonstrable genealogical relationship with any other language. That is, an isolate is a language family that only contains one member.

##### How are we defining 'endangered' languages?

The endangered statuses of languages, based on a six-point scale called the [Agglomerated Endangerment Scale (AES)](https://glottolog.org/langdoc/status), comes from [GlottoScope](https://glottolog.org/langdoc/status), which is a collection of global linguists dedicated to tracking endangered languages. The endangered statuses are as follows:

|   **AES status**   | AES Status Code | **\# of languages** | **% of languages** |
|:----------------:|:----------------:|:-----------------:|:----------------:|
| **Not Endangered** |        1        |        2770         |       35.34%       |
|   **Threatened**   |        2        |        1565         |       19.96%       |
|    **Shifting**    |        3        |        1799         |       22.95%       |
|    **Moribund**    |        4        |         421         |       5.37%        |
| **Nearly Extinct** |        5        |         300         |       3.83%        |
|    **Extinct**     |        6        |         984         |       12.55%       |
|     **total:**     |                 |        7839         |                    |

### Data Wrangling

#### Load Primary Data

There are three primary datasets from [TidyTuesday](https://github.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/blob/main/data/2025/2025-12-23/readme.md "The Languages of the World"), each of which are derived from [Glottolog](https://glottolog.org/), which is an open-access linguistics database.

The focus of the data are the languages spoken across the world. Primarily where they are spoken (both in terms of macroarea and lat/lon coordinates), and which families they belong to.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Download raw data and filter to endangered status
endangered_status <- 
  readr::read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/glottolog/glottolog-cldf/refs/heads/master/cldf/values.csv") |> 
  dplyr::filter(Parameter_ID == "aes") |> 
  dplyr::select(Language_ID, Value, Code_ID) |> 
  dplyr::rename(id = Language_ID,
                status_code = Value,
                status_label = Code_ID) |> 
  dplyr::mutate(status_label = stringr::str_replace(stringr::str_remove(status_label, "^aes-"), "_", " "))

# Download language and family data
fam_lgs <- 
  readr::read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/glottolog/glottolog-cldf/refs/heads/master/cldf/languages.csv")

# Filter and clean language family data
families <- 
  fam_lgs |> 
  dplyr::filter(Level == "family") |> 
  dplyr::select(ID, Name) |> 
  dplyr::rename(Family = Name) |> 
  dplyr::rename_with(stringr::str_to_lower, dplyr::everything())

# Filter and clean language data
languages <- 
  fam_lgs |> 
  dplyr::filter(Level == "language") |> 
  dplyr::select(ID, Name, Macroarea, Latitude, Longitude, ISO639P3code, Countries, Is_Isolate, Family_ID) |> 
  dplyr::rename_with(stringr::str_to_lower, dplyr::everything())
```
:::


#### Prepare Data


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Create master languages data frame
master_languages <- left_join(
  languages,
  endangered_status,
  by = "id"
) |>
  left_join(
    families,
    by     = c("family_id" = "id"),
    suffix = c("", "_name")
  ) |>
  filter(
    if_all(c(
      latitude, longitude, countries, status_code, status_label
      ), ~ !is.na(.x))
    ) |>
  mutate(
    status_label = str_to_title(status_label),
    status_label = factor(status_label, levels = c(
    "Not Endangered", "Threatened", "Shifting", 
    "Moribund", "Nearly Extinct", "Extinct")),
    not_endangered = if_else(
      status_label == "Not Endangered", "Not Endangered", "Threatened or Worse")
    )
```
:::


### Visualization


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(master_languages, aes(
  x = status_label, 
  fill = is_isolate
  )) + 
  geom_bar(position = "fill") + 
  labs(
    title    = "Proportion of Isolate Languages by Endangered Status",
    subtitle = "Stacked to 100%",
    x        = "Endangered Status Designation",
    y        = "Proportion of Languages",
    fill     = "Is Isolate",
    caption  = "Source: "
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 15),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 12),
    plot.caption  = element_text(hjust = 0)
  )
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/q1p1-plotting-1.png){fig-align='center' width=2700}
:::
:::


### Analysis & Discussion

## Part 2 - Largest Geographic Reach

Which language families span the largest geographic range?

Answering this question is only possible with a functional definition of 'largest geographic range'. I considered two different options:

1.  Haversine Distance, or essentially a point A to point B line between the two farthest apart speakers
2.  Bounding Box Area, which takes the min and max of latitude and longitude of speakers and creates a rectangle/area

### Data Wrangling

#### Calculate Custom Global Reach Metrics


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
# --- Option 1: Max Pairwise Haversine Distance ---
global_reach_haversine <- function(lons, lats) {
  coords <- cbind(lons, lats)
  if (nrow(coords) < 2) return(NA_real_)
  
  # distm returns a matrix of pairwise distances in meters
  dist_matrix <- geosphere::distm(coords, fun = distHaversine)
  max(dist_matrix, na.rm = TRUE) / 1000  # return in km
}

# --- Option 2: Bounding Box Area ---
global_reach_bbox <- function(lons, lats) {
  lat_range <- diff(range(lats, na.rm = TRUE))
  lon_range <- diff(range(lons, na.rm = TRUE))
  lat_range * lon_range  # in decimal degrees²
}


family_reach <- master_languages |>
  mutate(family = replace_na(family, "Isolate")) |>
  group_by(family) |>
  filter(!family %in% c(
  "Unattested", "Bookkeeping", "Unclassifiable",
  "Speech Register", "Pidgin", "Mixed Language"
  # "Artificial Language", "Sign Language", "Isolate"
  )) |>
  summarise(
    reach_km         = global_reach_haversine(longitude, latitude),
    reach_bbox_deg2  = global_reach_bbox(longitude, latitude),
    min_lat          = min(latitude, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_lat          = max(latitude, na.rm = TRUE),
    min_lon          = min(longitude, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_lon          = max(longitude, na.rm = TRUE),
    bb_asp           = diff(range(latitude, na.rm = TRUE)) / diff(range(longitude, na.rm = TRUE)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(reach_bbox_deg2))

top_10_reach <- family_reach |>
  slice_head(n = 10)
```
:::


### Visualization


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Create world map
world_map <- 
  ggplot() +
  geom_polygon(
    data = map_data("world"), 
    aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group),
    fill = "gray90", color = "white", linewidth = 0.1) + 
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL) + 
  coord_map(
    projection = "mercator",
    xlim = c(-180, 180),
    ylim = c(-50, 70)
  ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    panel.grid   = element_blank(),
    panel.border = element_blank(),
    axis.text    = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks   = element_blank()
  )

world_map + 
  geom_rect(
    data = top_10_reach,
    aes(xmin  = min_lon, xmax = max_lon, 
        ymin  = min_lat, ymax = max_lat,
        color = family, 
        fill  = after_scale(alpha(color, 0.3))),
    linewidth = 0.8
  ) + 
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "darkgray", linewidth = 0.5, linetype = "dotted") + 
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, color = "darkgray", linewidth = 0.5, linetype = "dotted") + 
  facet_wrap(~ family, ncol = 2) + 
  labs(
    title    = "Global Reach of Top 10 Language Families",
    subtitle = "Total Area of Recorded Speakers",
    caption  = "Source: 'The Languages of the World' TidyTuesday dataset, curated from Glottolog 5.2.1",
    color    = "Family"
       ) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title      = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 15),
    plot.subtitle   = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 12),
    panel.spacing   = unit(1, "cm"),
    strip.text      = element_text_repel(hjust = 0.5, size = 11)
  )
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-1-1.png){fig-align='center' width=3000}
:::
:::



::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(top_10_reach, aes(x = reorder(family, reach_km), y = reach_km)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#3d1a8e") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Global Reach of Top 10 Language Families",
    subtitle = "By Longest Distance (km) Between Recorded Speakers",
    caption  = "Source: 'The Languages of the World' TidyTuesday dataset, curated from Glottolog 5.2.1",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Haversine Line Distance (km)"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
  )
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/top-10-global-reach-1.png){fig-align='center' width=2700}
:::
:::


### Analysis & Discussion

It's unconventional, and maybe crude, to use a rectangular area (calculated from extremes) to measure the global reach of a language family.

#### Challenges & Solutions

I think the biggest challenge with this visualization is semantic: language family designations don't really mean much to us. While most Americans might guess that Indo-European is 'what we speak', I don't think languages' families are common knowledge.

# Question 2 - Socioeconomic Change vs. Linguistic Change

The point of Question 2 is to focus on languages that have an endangered status of 'Shifting'. Specifically, I was curious if there was any visible connection between a country's density of shifting languages and its socioeconomic change over the last 20 years. There are two proxies that I decided to use as measures of socioeconomic change:

1.  Average change in **GDP** over the last 20 years (2004 - 2024)
2.  Average change in **population** over the last 20 years (2004 - 2024)

### Data Wrangling

#### Load External Source Data


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
# World Bank aggregate codes to exclude from gdp dataset
wb_aggregates <- c("AFE", "AFW", "ARB", "CEB", "CHI", "CSS", "EAP", "EAR", 
                   "EAS", "ECA", "ECS", "EMU", "EUU", "FCS", "HIC", "HPC", 
                   "IBD", "IBT", "IDA", "IDB", "IDX", "INX", "LAC", "LCN", 
                   "LDC", "LIC", "LMC", "LMY", "LTE", "MEA", "MIC", "MNA", 
                   "NAC", "OED", "OSS", "PRE", "PSS", "PST", "SAS", "SSA", 
                   "SSF", "SST", "TEA", "TEC", "TLA", "TMN", "TSA", "TSS", 
                   "UMC", "WLD", "XKX")

# Gross domestic product, reformatted
gdp <- 
  read_csv("data/GDP_by_country_time.csv", skip = 4) |>
  select(-`Indicator Name`, -`Indicator Code`) |>
  rename(country = `Country Name`, country_code = `Country Code`) |>
  filter(!country_code %in% wb_aggregates) |>
  select(where(~!all(is.na(.)))) |>  
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -c(country, country_code),
    names_to = "year",
    values_to = "gdp_growth_yoy"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    year = as.integer(year),
    gdp_growth_yoy = as.numeric(gdp_growth_yoy),
    country_code = countrycode(
      country_code, 
      origin = "iso3c", 
      destination = "iso2c",
      custom_match = c("XKX" = "XK")
    )
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(gdp_growth_yoy)) |>
  filter(year >= 2004) |>
  arrange(country)

# Population change and density data by country
pop <- 
  read_csv("data/unpopulation_dataportal.csv") |>
  filter(
    Time >= 2004, 
    IndicatorName == "Rate of population change",
    Variant == "Median",
    Sex == "Both sexes"
  ) |>
  select("IndicatorName", "Location", "Iso2", "Iso3", "Time", "Value") |>
  rename(year = "Time", country = "Location", country_code = "Iso2") |>
  select(where(~!all(is.na(.)))) |> 
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = "IndicatorName",
    values_from = "Value"
  ) |>
  rename(pop_change_yoy = "Rate of population change") |>
  mutate(
    year = as.integer(year),
    pop_change_yoy = as.numeric(pop_change_yoy)
  )

# UN developing countries list
dev_countries <- 
  read_csv("data/un_developing_countries_data.csv") |>
  mutate(country_code = countrycode(
    country,
    origin = "country.name",                                
    destination = "iso2c",
    custom_match = c(
      "Democratic Rep. of the Congo"      = "CD",
      "Lao People's Dem. Republic"        = "LA",
      "Plurinational State of Bolivia"    = "BO",
      "Micronesia (Federated States of)"  = "FM",
      "São Tomé and Príncipe"             = "ST",
      "Timor-Leste"                       = "TL"
    )
  ))
```
:::


#### Prepare Data


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Separating delimited countries & joining w/ custom datasets
lang_country_change <- master_languages |>
  filter(!is.na(countries)) |>
  select(-iso639p3code) |>
  separate_rows(countries, sep = ";") |>  # one row per country per language
  rename(country_code = "countries") |>
  mutate(
    status_code = as.integer(status_code)
  ) |>
  left_join(
    gdp,
    by = "country_code"
  ) |>
  left_join(
    pop |> select(-country, -Iso3),
    by = c("country_code", "year")
  )

# Separating delimited countries & joining w/ custom datasets
master_countries <- lang_country_change |>
  group_by(country, country_code, status_label, status_code) |>
  summarise(
    n_languages = n_distinct(id), 
    avg_annual_gdp_change_20_year = mean(gdp_growth_yoy, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_annual_pop_change_20_year = mean(pop_change_yoy, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
    ) |>
  group_by(country, country_code) |>
  mutate(
    total_languages   = sum(n_languages),
    status_proportion = n_languages / total_languages,
    avg_status_score  = mean(status_code)
  )

# Iteration of master countries with only shifting languages
master_countries_shifting <- master_countries |>
  filter(status_label == "Shifting")

# Merging external data with world map data
country_economics <- master_countries |>
  distinct(country, country_code, 
           avg_annual_gdp_change_20_year, 
           avg_annual_pop_change_20_year
  )

world <- ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf")

map_data_status <- world |>
  left_join(master_countries_shifting, by = c("iso_a2" = "country_code"))

map_data_econ <- world |>
  left_join(country_economics, by = c("iso_a2" = "country_code"))

map_ldc_countries <- world |>
  left_join(dev_countries, by = c("iso_a2" = "country_code")) |>
  filter(iso_a2 %in% dev_countries$country_code)
```
:::


### Visualization

My plan for visualizing this story was to use repetition as a tool. I wanted to begin by introducing a rather obscure metric, shifting language density, as its own plot to familiar the viewer. Then, as I move through the different socioeconomic elements of the question, I wanted to keep the same map and format but change the color palette so the viewer understands that the metric has changed. Beyond that, I'm reprinting the initial map because I think it's easier to view the patterns of each metric side by side.

#### Shifting Language Density

The first step is to highlight countries with the highest concentration of shifting languages. This is calculated per country as the percent of total languages spoken that have an endangered status of `Shifting`.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Patch 1: shifting languages
p1 <- ggplot(map_data_status) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = status_proportion), color = "white", linewidth = 0.1) +
  coord_sf(ylim = c(-60, 85), expand = FALSE) +
  geom_label_repel(
    data = map_data_status |> filter(name %in% c(
      "New Zealand",
      "Costa Rica",
      "Nepal",
      "Greenland",
      "Sweden"
      )), 
    aes(label = name, geometry = geometry),
    stat               = "sf_coordinates",
    size               = 4,
    min.segment.length = 0,
    nudge_x            = c(-15, -15, -20, -10, -5),
    nudge_y            = c(-14, 8, 0, -10, -6),
    segment.color      = "grey40",
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    option    = "rocket", 
    direction = -1, 
    na.value  = "grey90",
    labels    = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Global 'Shifting' Language Density", 
    subtitle = "By Percent of Countries' Total Languages",
    fill     = "'Shifting' Language Density",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title             = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 15),
    plot.subtitle          = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 12),
    legend.box.background  = element_rect(fill = "white", color = "black", 
                                          linewidth = 0.35, alpha = 0.8),
    legend.position        = "inside",
    legend.position.inside = c(.15, .18),
    legend.direction       = "horizontal",
    legend.title.position  = "top",
    legend.title           = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 8),
    legend.text            = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 7)
  )
p1
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/p1-shifting-language-density-1.png){fig-align='center' width=3600}
:::
:::


##### Anti-Patterns

What stands out most to me about this visual is just how few patterns there are in shifting language density around the world. The only thing that really stands out is that Central and South America as a whole seem to have more consistently shifting languages than the rest of the world.

But, aside from South America, I wanted to highlight some of the densest countries of shifting languages. These five countries are shockingly diverse, differing in size, population, development, and even continent.

#### Shifting Languages vs. Shifting Economy

What relationship exists between shifting/endangered languages and average GDP change of the countries in which it's spoken (last 20 years).

##### Violin Plot


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(master_countries, aes(x = status_label, 
                             y = avg_annual_gdp_change_20_year, 
                             color = status_label)) +
  geom_violin() +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.1, alpha = 0.3, size = 1) + 
  labs(
    title    = "Language Endangered Status vs. Avg. Annual Change in GDP",
    subtitle = "GDP Average: 20 Year Window (2004 - 2024)",
    y        = "Average Annual Change in GDP",
    x        = "Endangered Status"
  ) + 
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1, scale = 1)) +
  guides(color = guide_legend(reverse = TRUE)) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 15),
    plot.subtitle   = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 12),
    legend.position = "none"
  )
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/gdp-violin-plot-1.png){fig-align='center' width=2700}
:::
:::


##### 


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Patch 1b: removing country labels
p1b <- ggplot(map_data_status) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = status_proportion), color = "white", linewidth = 0.1) +
  coord_sf(ylim = c(-60, 85), expand = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    option    = "rocket", 
    direction = -1, 
    na.value  = "grey90",
    labels    = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Global 'Shifting' Language Density", 
    subtitle = "By Percent of Countries' Total Languages",
    fill     = "'Shifting' Language Density",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title             = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 15),
    plot.subtitle          = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 12),
    legend.box.background  = element_rect(fill = "white", color = "black", 
                                          linewidth = 0.35, alpha = 0.8),
    legend.position        = "inside",
    legend.position.inside = c(.15, .18),
    legend.direction       = "horizontal",
    legend.title.position  = "top",
    legend.title           = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 8),
    legend.text            = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 7)
  )

# Patch 2: gdp data
p2 <- ggplot(map_data_econ) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = avg_annual_gdp_change_20_year), color = "white", linewidth = 0.1) +
  coord_sf(ylim = c(-60, 85), expand = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    option = "mako", 
    direction = -1, 
    na.value = "grey90",
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1, scale = 1)
    ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Shifting Economies", 
    subtitle = "Average Annual Change in GDP (2004 - 2024)",
    fill     = "Avg. Annual GDP Change"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title            = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 15),
    plot.subtitle         = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 12),
    legend.box.background = element_rect(
      fill = "white", color = "black", 
      linewidth = 0.35, alpha = 0.8),
    legend.position        = "inside",
    legend.position.inside = c(.15, .18),
    legend.direction       = "horizontal",
    legend.title.position  = "top",
    legend.title           = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 8),
    legend.text            = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 7)
  )
p1b / p2
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/p2-gdp-map-1.png){fig-align='center' width=3600}
:::
:::


#### Shifting Languages vs. Shifting Population

What relationship exists between shifting/endangered languages and the average population change of the countries in which it's spoken (last 20 years).


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Patch 3: population data
p3 <- ggplot(map_data_econ) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = avg_annual_pop_change_20_year), color = "white", linewidth = 0.1) +
  coord_sf(ylim = c(-60, 85), expand = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    option = "mako", 
    direction = -1, 
    na.value = "grey90",
    labels    = percent_format(accuracy = 1, scale = 1),
    ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Shifting Populations", 
    subtitle = "Average Annual Change in Population (2004 - 2024)",
    fill     = "Avg. Annual Population Change"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title            = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 15),
    plot.subtitle         = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 12),
    legend.box.background = element_rect(
      fill = "white", color = "black", 
      linewidth = 0.35),
    legend.position        = "inside",
    legend.position.inside = c(.15, .18),
    legend.direction       = "horizontal",
    legend.title.position  = "top",
    legend.title           = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 8),
    legend.text            = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 7)
  )
p1b / p3
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/p3-shifting-population-1.png){fig-align='center' width=3600}
:::
:::


#### Shifting Languages vs. UN-Designated Developing Countries


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Patch 4: least developed country data
p4 <- ggplot(map_data_status) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = status_proportion), color = "white", linewidth = 0.1) +
  geom_sf(data = map_ldc_countries, fill = NA, color = "#00E5E5", linewidth = 0.75) +
  coord_sf(ylim = c(-60, 85), expand = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    option    = "rocket", 
    direction = -1, 
    na.value  = "grey90",
    labels    = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Shifting Languages vs. United Nations Least Developed Countries",
    subtitle = "Color = Shifting Language Density | Highlighting = Least Developed Countries",
    caption  = "Source: https://www.un.org/ohrlls/content/list-ldcs",
    fill     = "Percent 'Shifting'"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title            = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 15),
    plot.subtitle         = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 12),
    legend.box.background = element_rect(
      fill = "white", color = "black", 
      linewidth = 0.35, alpha = 0.8),
    legend.position        = "inside",
    legend.position.inside = c(.15, .18),
    legend.direction       = "horizontal",
    legend.title.position  = "top",
    legend.title           = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 8),
    legend.text            = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 7)
  )
p4
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/p4-least-developed-countries-1.png){fig-align='center' width=3600}
:::
